case_name: Lake water potable reuse with mixed cyanide, propoxur, and strontium under low-complexity and residuals constraints
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for lake water intended for potable reuse containing strontium, cyanide, and propoxur while balancing compliance reliability against cost, operator simplicity, and residuals burden.

input_data.scenario_background: A regional utility plans to blend and upgrade impacted lake water as part of a potable reuse scheme after episodic inflow from urban drainage and legacy industrial shoreline areas. The main challenge is that cyanide and propoxur require robust destructive or adsorptive treatment, while strontium is more effectively controlled by membrane or selective ion-exchange routes that increase cost or residuals complexity. The utility has limited specialist staffing and wants to avoid high-difficulty brine or concentrate disposal, so the best choice is not simply the most technically aggressive train but the one that best balances reliable contaminant control with manageable O&M.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve stable potable reuse quality with cyanide and propoxur reduced to low, consistently compliant levels and strontium lowered sufficiently to support a conservative finished-water risk management target.
secondary_goals: avoid difficult concentrate or regenerant disposal where possible
maintain resilience to seasonal lake water quality swings
control taste, odor, and organic precursor carryover as co-benefits

engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: treatment should tolerate seasonal turbidity and organic matter variation without highly specialized operator intervention

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove suspended solids and lower organic loading ahead of oxidation and adsorption
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: provide particulate polishing and protect downstream units
core_unit_functions.ozonation: oxidize cyanide and transform propoxur into more biodegradable intermediates while improving downstream BAC performance
core_unit_functions.biological_activated_carbon: adsorb and biologically polish pesticide residues and oxidation byproducts while reducing DOC
core_unit_functions.selective_cation_exchange_polishing: provide targeted strontium reduction without full desalination
core_unit_functions.UV_disinfection: final pathogen barrier for potable reuse

applicability_rationale: this train balances the mixed contaminant problem by using oxidation and BAC for cyanide and propoxur, then a limited polishing step for strontium rather than relying on an expensive full membrane barrier
conventional solids removal is well suited to variable lake water and improves the stability of downstream ozone and BAC units
BAC offers co-benefits for organic matter and taste-odor control that are valuable in potable reuse lake water applications
a dedicated cation-exchange polishing step can address moderate strontium levels with lower energy and smaller concentrate burden than RO
overall complexity is higher than conventional treatment alone but still more manageable than full high-pressure membrane treatment
potential_risks: cyanide oxidation performance depends on dose control, pH, and contact time
BAC exhaustion or biofilter upsets can reduce propoxur polishing reliability
cation exchange introduces resin replacement or regeneration management needs
if strontium targets become very stringent this route may be less robust than RO

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: reduce turbidity and NOM to stabilize downstream treatment
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: remove carryover solids and protect adsorption units
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon: provide flexible control of episodic pesticide loading
core_unit_functions.chlorination_for_cyanide_oxidation: oxidize free cyanide under controlled conditions
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: polish propoxur and residual oxidizable organics

applicability_rationale: this is a relatively familiar and lower-complexity train for utilities already operating conventional treatment
PAC plus GAC can provide practical propoxur control and help buffer episodic spikes
controlled chlorination is a recognized route for cyanide oxidation in appropriate waters
the route ranks below the top option because it does not directly provide strong strontium removal, so it is only attractive if blending or source management can help meet the strontium objective
potential_risks: limited inherent strontium reduction may leave compliance margin too narrow
chlorination for cyanide control requires careful pH and dose management and can form chlorinated byproducts if misapplied
PAC handling and spent carbon management add residuals and housekeeping demands
higher DOC events can shorten GAC run time

core_unit_functions.ultrafiltration: provide fine particulate and colloid removal for RO protection
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: remove dissolved strontium and provide strong barrier to many dissolved contaminants
core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation_process: destroy residual trace organics such as propoxur and polish oxidizable contaminants including cyanide species remaining after RO train management
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust corrosivity and mineral balance of permeate

applicability_rationale: technically this is the most comprehensive route for simultaneous dissolved contaminant control and is highly compatible with potable reuse expectations
RO is the most reliable option in the list for meaningful strontium reduction and also offers broad co-removal of other dissolved species
the route ranks only third because its cost, energy demand, membrane fouling management, and concentrate disposal conflict with the stated constraints
potential_risks: membrane concentrate handling is a major challenge under the residuals constraint
higher operator skill is needed for membrane integrity, cleaning, and scaling control
RO removes minerals and requires stabilization before distribution or blending
capital intensity may be difficult to justify for a moderate strontium concentration

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove particulates and some organic precursors
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: polish solids and protect downstream oxidation and adsorption units
core_unit_functions.ozonation: oxidize cyanide and improve biodegradability of organic contaminants
core_unit_functions.lime_soda_softening: partially reduce hardness-related ions and achieve some incidental strontium removal through precipitation/coprecipitation

applicability_rationale: this route uses familiar unit processes and avoids membrane concentrate generation
softening can provide some strontium reduction, especially where precipitation and solids capture are well controlled
it ranks lower because strontium removal is less predictable and generally weaker than selective ion exchange or RO, while softening increases sludge production and chemical handling
potential_risks: strontium reduction may be insufficient for a conservative potable reuse target
softening sludge generation conflicts with residuals management preferences
chemical feed control is more involved than purely adsorptive polishing
GAC replacement frequency may increase during high organic load periods

core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: protect advanced units from solids carryover
core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation_process: oxidatively degrade propoxur and oxidize cyanide species

applicability_rationale: this route is included as a technically plausible but poorly aligned option
AOP can be effective for some trace organics and may contribute to cyanide destruction, but it is relatively energy intensive and operationally sensitive
anion exchange is not the preferred mechanism for strontium removal compared with cation-selective exchange or RO, so the overall mixed-contaminant fit is weak
potential_risks: misalignment between anion exchange chemistry and cationic strontium removal objective
AOP performance can be depressed by lake water scavenging from DOC and alkalinity
chemical and energy demand may be hard to justify under budget constraints
spent resin or regenerant handling still creates residual management issues

preferred_rank_pattern: integrated oxidation_plus_carbon_plus_targeted_strontium_polishing routes should rank near the top
a lower-complexity conventional_plus_adsorption route should appear as a strong alternative even if strontium control is weaker
RO_based treatment should appear as technically strong but not rank first under cost and residuals constraints
an option with chemistry misaligned to strontium removal should rank near the bottom

must_reflect: tradeoff between strong dissolved strontium control and avoiding membrane concentrate disposal
preference for utility-familiar and lower-complexity operations
importance of residuals burden from sludge, spent carbon, regenerant, or concentrate

must_contain: why oxidation and carbon-based steps are relevant for cyanide and propoxur in lake water
why strontium is harder to address with conventional treatment alone
why RO is downgraded despite broad technical effectiveness
why selective cation exchange is a compromise option for moderate strontium reduction

should_mention: spent media sludge regenerant or concentrate handling
